displayName: Magnetic or Non-Magnetic? — Sort Twelve Everyday Objects
description: An interactive sorting activity for NCERT Curiosity Grade 6 Science, Chapter 9 (Methods of Separation in Everyday Life). Twelve everyday object cards appear in a shuffled grid: five magnetic objects (iron nail, steel safety pin, iron key, steel paper clip, steel spoon) and seven non-magnetic objects, three of which are metals (aluminium foil, copper coin, brass bell) and four non-metals (plastic ruler, rubber eraser, wooden block, glass marble). The student taps an object to select it, then taps one of two bins — 'Sticks to Magnet' or 'Does NOT Stick'. Correct placements lock in with a pop animation and show the material label; wrong placements shake and display a contextual hint about the material. A 'Metal Trap' callout counter highlights when the student correctly identifies a non-magnetic metal, targeting the common misconception that all metals are magnetic. On completion, a summary table lists all twelve objects by bin with their materials, followed by the key insight ('Not all metals are magnetic — only those containing iron, nickel, or cobalt'), the textbook definition of magnetic separation, and a textbook connection to the carpenter-and-sawdust story.
subject: Science
chapter: Chapter 9 — Methods of Separation in Everyday Life
topic: Magnetic separation — separating magnetic from non-magnetic substances

curriculum.subject: Science
curriculum.chapter: 9. Methods of Separation in Everyday Life
curriculum.activity: Activity 9.6 — Let us play (sorting scenarios into separation methods)
learningObjectives: O23: Define magnetic substances as substances, such as iron, that are attracted towards a magnet
O24: Define magnetic separation as separating magnetic from non-magnetic substances by using a magnet

showHints.description: Show material-specific hints on wrong placements.

shuffleSeed.description: Seed for card shuffle order. Null uses Date.now() for random each session.

misconception.correction: Only metals containing iron, nickel, or cobalt are magnetic. Aluminium, copper, brass, gold, silver, and zinc are metals but are NOT attracted by a magnet.
misconception.toolFeature: Three of the twelve objects are non-magnetic metals (aluminium foil, copper coin, brass bell). A 'Metal Trap' counter appears when students correctly place these, reinforcing that being a metal does not guarantee being magnetic.

ncertAlignment.textbookStory: A carpenter drops iron nails in sawdust; a magnet is moved through the sawdust and all the nails get attracted. Substances attracted towards a magnet are called magnetic substances. Iron is a common example. Separation of magnetic and non-magnetic substances by using a magnet is called magnetic separation.
ncertAlignment.enrichment: Recyclers use magnets fitted to cranes to separate scrap iron from waste heaps. The scrap iron can be recycled and reused.
